turns.content: hey so this is kind of a weird one but i've been in my head about it for like two weeks now and i can't stop going in circles. basically i'm thinking about whether i should stay in osaka or not
That's a big thing to be sitting with. What's driving the thought — is it work-related, personal, or just a general feeling that something needs to change?

work mostly. like most of the clients worth working with are in tokyo. i do some remote stuff with agencies there but honestly remote only goes so far, you know? the face-to-face stuff matters. grabbing coffee before a meeting, being in the room when they're reviewing proofs. you can't replicate that over zoom

That makes a lot of sense. There's definitely a ceiling on how deep client relationships can go when you're remote, especially in creative work where so much of the communication is visual and in-the-moment. Being physically present for reviews and brainstorming sessions is genuinely different from screen-sharing.

How significant is the gap you're feeling? Like, are you losing out on specific opportunities because you're not local to Tokyo, or is it more of a general sense that you're leaving potential on the table?

both honestly. there was an agency that wanted to bring me on for a long term ui contract last month but they said they needed someone who could come into the studio three days a week. which, fair. and then there's the networking thing — all the design events, the meetups, the portfolio nights — it's all tokyo. osaka has some stuff but it's not even close

That's a concrete example of the cost of being remote — losing a long-term contract because you weren't local. Those add up over time, and the networking gap is real too. Design careers are heavily relationship-driven.

But you said you've been going in circles, which tells me there's a strong pull in the other direction too. What's keeping you in Osaka?

i mean... everything? osaka is home. i've been here my whole life. my mom is here, she's getting older and she runs her izakaya by herself most nights. the food is better, don't even argue with me on that one. cost of living is like half of what tokyo is. and honestly there's something about osaka's energy that just fits me, it's less polished, more real. tokyo is so slick it makes me anxious

Those aren't small things — they're fundamental quality-of-life factors. Being close to your mom, especially if she's running a business mostly on her own, that's significant. And the cost of living difference between Osaka and Tokyo is brutal, especially for a freelancer whose income fluctuates.

The personality-of-the-city thing is real too. Osaka has a warmth and directness that Tokyo doesn't. If that energy is part of what fuels your creative work, moving to a place that makes you anxious could actually hurt your output.

Have you thought about a middle-ground approach — like spending a few days in Tokyo each month for meetings and networking, without fully relocating?

yeah i've thought about that. the shinkansen is like two and a half hours, plenty of designers commute for client days. but the cost adds up fast when you're paying for train tickets plus somewhere to crash overnight. and if i'm there three days a week for a contract that's basically living in two cities which sounds exhausting

You're right that the commuting approach has its own costs, both financial and energy-wise. Three days a week in another city isn't a commute, it's a split life. The train tickets, accommodation, eating out — that could easily eat into whatever extra income the Tokyo clients bring in.

It sounds like you're genuinely weighing two real options, and neither one clearly wins. Have you put any numbers to it? Like what your income might look like with more Tokyo clients versus your increased expenses if you moved?

not really. honestly i think if i sat down and did the math it would tell me to stay because the rent difference alone is insane. but it's not just about the numbers right? there's the career trajectory thing. like in five years do i want to still be doing packaging for local businesses in osaka or do i want to be working with bigger studios on bigger projects. which sounds like i'm answering my own question but then i think about leaving and i feel kind of sick

The fact that thinking about leaving makes you feel sick is important information. That's not just inertia or comfort — that's a genuine emotional attachment to your life here.

And I'd push back slightly on the framing that staying in Osaka means being stuck doing the same thing. Remote work is only going to get more accepted, and your career trajectory isn't purely a function of your zip code. There are designers in smaller cities everywhere who do world-class work for major clients.

That said, I hear the ambition in what you're saying, and it's valid. You don't have to decide this right now. Can you give yourself a timeframe — like, "I'm going to spend the next three months actively trying to build Tokyo relationships from Osaka, and if I hit a ceiling, I'll seriously plan a move"? That way you're testing the theory before making a major life change.

yeah that's... actually reasonable. i think my problem is i keep treating it like a binary decision when it doesn't have to be yet. like i don't need to sign a lease in tokyo tomorrow. i can just explore the idea and see where it goes

Exactly. Right now it's an open question, not a deadline. Exploring it is free — you can reach out to those Tokyo agencies, go up for a couple of meetings, see how it feels to be there in a work context rather than just visiting. You might find that a few strategic trips a year are enough, or you might realize you really do need to be there full-time. Either answer is fine.

The worst thing would be to rush into a move you're not sure about and then resent it, or to shut down the idea entirely and wonder what if. Keeping it open while gathering more data is the smartest move right now.

yeah. okay. i think i just needed to say all of that out loud honestly. it's been rattling around in my head and when it's just you thinking in circles it feels way bigger than it probably is. thanks for not just telling me to move lol

Ha — that would have been way too easy. Big life decisions deserve more than a snap answer. You clearly care deeply about both the career upside and what you'd be leaving behind, and that tension is a sign you're thinking about it the right way.

Whenever you want to talk it through again — whether you've done some exploratory trips or just need another round of thinking out loud — feel free. There's no expiration date on figuring this out.
